iti91:1.2: This was said by the Blessed One, said by the Arahant, so I have heard:
iti91:1.3:
"This is a lowly means of livelihood, alms gathering. It's a form of abuse in the world
, 'You go around as an alms gatherer with a bowl in your hand!' Yet sensible young men of good families have taken it up for a compelling reason. They have not been forced into it by kings or robbers, nor through debt, through fear, nor through the loss of their livelihood, but through the thought: 'We are beset by birth, aging, and death, by sorrows, lamentations, pains, suffering, and despairs, beset by suffering, overcome with suffering. O, that the end of this entire mass of suffering might be known!' But this young man of good family, having gone forth in this way, may be greedy for sensual pleasures, strong in his passions, malevolent in mind, corrupt in his resolves, his mindfulness muddled, unalert, uncentered, his mind scattered, and his faculties uncontrolled. Just as a firebrand from a funeral pyre — burning at both ends, covered with excrement in the middle — is used as fuel neither in a village nor in the wilderness: I tell you that this is a simile for this person. He has missed out on the householder's enjoyments and does not fulfill the purpose of the contemplative life."
iti91:1.4: He's missed out on the householder's enjoyment and the purpose of the contemplative life — unfortunate man! Ruining it, he throws it away, perishes like a firebrand used at a funeral.
iti91:1.5: Better to eat an iron ball — glowing, aflame — than that, unprincipled and unrestrained, he should eat the alms of the country.
iti91:1.6: This, too, was the meaning of what was said by the Blessed One, so I have heard.
